name: Identity Taxonomy
description: The root of the personal identity map.

children.name: Sexuality & Romantic Orientation
children.description: The root category for sexual and romantic orientations.

children.name: A-Spectrum
children.description: An umbrella term for those who experience little to no sexual or romantic attraction, or experience it under specific circumstances.

children.name: Asexual (Ace)
children.description: Little to no sexual attraction to anyone.

children.name: Aromantic (Aro)
children.description: Little to no romantic attraction to anyone.

children.name: Aroace
children.description: Experiencing little to no romantic and sexual attraction; identifying as both aromantic and asexual.

children.name: Demisexual / Demiromantic
children.description: Feeling attraction only after forming a deep emotional connection.

children.name: Graysexual / Grayromantic
children.description: Falling in the 'gray area' between asexual/aromantic and allosexual/alloromantic; experiencing attraction rarely or weakly.

children.name: Lithosexual / Lithromantic
children.description: Feeling attraction to others but not wanting those feelings reciprocated.

children.name: Cupiosexual / Cupioromantic
children.description: Desiring a sexual or romantic relationship despite not experiencing the corresponding attraction.

children.name: Reciprosexual
children.description: Experiencing attraction only after realizing someone is already attracted to them.

children.name: Apothisexual
children.description: Being sex-repulsed while identifying on the asexual spectrum.

children.name: Multisexual (M-Spec)
children.description: An umbrella term for anyone attracted to multiple genders.

children.name: Bisexual (Bi)
children.description: Attraction to two or more genders.

children.name: Pansexual (Pan)
children.description: Attraction to people regardless of their gender; often called 'gender-blind' attraction.

children.name: Omnisexual
children.description: Attraction to people of all genders, where gender is actively recognized as part of the attraction.

children.name: Polysexual
children.description: Attraction to multiple, but not necessarily all, genders.

children.name: Spectrasexual
children.description: Attraction to multiple, but not all, sexes, genders, and gender identities.

children.name: Abrosexual
children.description: Having a sexuality that is fluid and changes frequently over time.

children.name: Monosexual
children.description: An umbrella term for anyone attracted to only one gender.

children.name: Gay
children.description: Attraction to the same or similar gender; traditionally used by men, but serves as a broad umbrella.

children.name: Lesbian
children.description: Women, non-binary, or gender-nonconforming people attracted to the same or similar genders.

children.name: Androsexual
children.description: Attraction to masculinity, male anatomy, or people who identify as men.

children.name: Gynosexual
children.description: Attraction to femininity, female anatomy, or people who identify as women.

children.name: Ceterosexual
children.description: Attraction exclusively to non-binary, genderqueer, or gender-nonconforming people.

children.name: Other Orientations
children.description: Other specific expressions of sexual or romantic orientation.

children.name: Allosexual / Alloromantic
children.description: Someone who experiences sexual or romantic attraction (the opposite of asexual/aromantic).

children.name: Fraysexual
children.description: Attraction that is strong at first but fades as an emotional bond forms (the opposite of demisexual).

children.name: Sapiosexual
children.description: Attraction based primarily on intelligence rather than gender or physical appearance.

children.name: Autosexual / Autoromantic
children.description: Experiencing sexual or romantic attraction primarily or exclusively toward oneself.

children.name: Pomosexual
children.description: Rejecting sexual orientation labels entirely, or feeling that existing labels do not fit.

children.name: Queer
children.description: A reclaimed, broad umbrella term for anyone outside societal norms of sexuality and gender.

children.name: Gender Identity
children.description: The root category for internal senses of gender.

children.name: Cisgender (Cis)
children.description: Identifying with the gender assigned at birth.

children.name: Transgender Spectrum
children.description: An umbrella term for anyone whose gender identity differs from the sex assigned at birth.

children.name: Transgender (Trans)
children.description: Identifying primarily with a gender different from the one assigned at birth.

children.name: Gender Non-Conforming (GNC)
children.description: Behaving or expressing gender in ways that do not align with traditional societal expectations.

children.name: Non-binary Spectrum
children.description: An umbrella term for any gender identity that sits outside the strict male/female binary.

children.name: Non-binary (Enby)
children.description: Identifying outside the strict male/female binary.

children.name: Agender
children.description: Having no gender, identifying as genderless, or a lack of gender identity.

children.name: Genderqueer
children.description: Rejecting traditional gender binaries and embracing fluid or non-traditional gender identities.

children.name: Genderfluid
children.description: A gender identity that changes, shifts, or fluctuates over time.

children.name: Genderflux
children.description: A gender identity that varies in intensity over time.

children.name: Demigender
children.description: Feeling a partial, but not full, connection to a specific gender (e.g., Demiboy/Demigirl).

children.name: Demiflux
children.description: Having a static part of one's gender and another part that fluctuates in intensity.

children.name: Cassgender
children.description: Feeling that one's gender is unimportant or feeling indifferent to the concept of gender.

children.name: Bigender
children.description: Experiencing two distinct gender identities, either at the same time or alternating.

children.name: Trigender
children.description: Experiencing three distinct gender identities.

children.name: Multigender / Pangender
children.description: Identifying with multiple, all, or countless genders simultaneously or over time.

children.name: Neutrois
children.description: A neutral or null gender identity.

children.name: Aporagender
children.description: A gender identity completely separate from binary genders, but still defined.

children.name: Maverique
children.description: A distinct, positive identity that is entirely independent of male, female, or neutrality.

children.name: Androgynous
children.description: Identifying as a mix of both masculine and feminine characteristics.

children.name: Novigender
children.description: A gender identity that is too complex to be described by existing terminology.

children.name: Aliagender
children.description: A gender identity that is distinct and separate from 'man' or 'woman'.

children.name: Xenogender Spectrum
children.description: Genders described using concepts outside typical human understandings of gender, such as nature, space, or symbols.

children.name: Xenogender
children.description: A gender categorized using non-traditional, metaphorical, or symbolic associations.

children.name: Astralgender
children.description: A gender that feels connected to space, stars, and the cosmos.

children.name: Catgender
children.description: A xenogender strongly connected to cats or felines, either aesthetically or conceptually.

children.name: Aesthetigender
children.description: A gender experience derived from, or acting as the embodiment of, a specific aesthetic.

children.name: Gendervoid
children.description: Identifying as having a void or empty space where a gender would be.

children.name: Culture-Specific & Historic Labels
children.description: Labels tied to specific cultural, racial, or historical contexts.

children.name: Two-Spirit
children.description: A modern umbrella term used by some Indigenous North Americans to describe a traditional third-gender or fluid role in their cultures.

children.name: Muxe
children.description: A recognized third gender in Zapotec cultures of Oaxaca, Mexico.

children.name: Hijra
children.description: A legally recognized third gender in South Asian cultures (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh).

children.name: Fa'afafine
children.description: A recognized third gender in Samoan culture, translating to 'in the manner of a woman.'

children.name: Mahu
children.description: In Kanaka Maoli (Hawaiian) and Maohi (Tahitian) cultures, people of third gender with traditional spiritual and social roles.

children.name: Kathoey
children.description: A trans woman or effeminate gay man in Thailand, historically recognized as a distinct demographic.

children.name: Stud / Femme
children.description: Identity labels historically used within Black and Latinx lesbian communities to describe specific presentations.

children.name: Relationship Structures
children.description: Terms describing variations in how individuals structure their relationships.

children.name: Polyamorous
children.description: Engaging in or desiring multiple romantic relationships simultaneously with the consent of all involved.

children.name: Monogamous
children.description: Engaging in romantic and sexual relationships with only one partner at a time.

children.name: Ambiamorous
children.description: Being equally comfortable in either a polyamorous or monogamous relationship structure.

children.name: Relationship Anarchy
children.description: The application of anarchist principles to relationships, prioritizing autonomy and avoiding rigid hierarchies or traditional relationship milestones.

children.name: Intersex & Altersex Experiences
children.description: Experiences related to biological sex characteristics and desired anatomy.

children.name: Intersex
children.description: An umbrella term for people born with biological sex characteristics that do not fit typical medical definitions of male or female.

children.name: Altersex
children.description: A catch-all term for anyone whose biological sex characteristics are outside the typical male/female binary, often through conscious modification.
